records.evidence: implementation landed: src/worker.py:3 `MAX_ATTEMPTS = 5`, src/worker.py:4 `TIMEOUT_S = 30`, src/worker.py:15-20 `handle_failure` (increment, drop at >= MAX_ATTEMPTS, else push_tail) match the design's Sketch section verbatim; Problem/Options/Sketch sections are superseded scaffolding

claims.claim: Exhausted jobs are dropped, never parked — a dead-letter queue was rejected because the service deploys unattended and an unmonitored DLQ grows without bound; revisit only if an operator rotation ever exists.
There is deliberately no backoff between attempts: re-entering at the tail already spaces retries by roughly the queue's drain time, so an explicit backoff schedule would double-delay.
claims.evidence: src/worker.py:20 `queue.push_tail(job)   # failed jobs re-enter at the tail`

claims.claim: The per-attempt timeout is one global `TIMEOUT_S` for every job type — per-type budgets were rejected because each new job type would become a tuning decision nobody owns; fast types wasting budget under the global cap is accepted.
claims.evidence: src/worker.py:4 `TIMEOUT_S = 30     # one global per-attempt timeout, all job types`

payload.decision_entry:
## 2026-02-14 — worker queue failure handling
- Decided: bounded retries (MAX_ATTEMPTS=5), failed jobs re-enter at the tail, drop on exhaustion; one global per-attempt TIMEOUT_S=30 for all job types.
- Rejected: dead-letter queue (system deploys unattended; an unmonitored DLQ grows without bound and hides failures); exponential backoff (tail re-entry already spaces attempts by queue drain time); per-job-type timeouts (each new type becomes a tuning decision nobody owns).
- Still binds: revisit a DLQ only if an operator rotation ever exists for this service.
- Code: src/worker.py (MAX_ATTEMPTS, TIMEOUT_S, handle_failure).
- Source: docs/plans/2026-02-14-worker-queue-design.md (retired in this distillation).

records.evidence: implementation landed: the plan's Task 2 code block is now src/worker.py:15-20 `handle_failure` verbatim, and the `attempts` field it adds is written at src/worker.py:16 `job.attempts += 1`; the task list is superseded scaffolding

claims.claim: Job records carry an `attempts` counter initialized to 0; `handle_failure` is its only writer, incrementing once per failure and dropping the job when `attempts >= MAX_ATTEMPTS` (the boundary is >=, so exactly 5 attempts run).

payload.decision_entry:
## 2026-03-01 — attempts counter on jobs
- Decided: per-job `attempts` int (default 0), incremented only in the failure path; drop at `attempts >= MAX_ATTEMPTS`.
- Code: src/worker.py:15-20 (handle_failure).
- Source: docs/plans/2026-03-01-attempts-field-plan.md (retired in this distillation).

records.evidence: docs/reference/queue-walkthrough.md:5-8 — four narrative sentences whose checkable content is one fact: `run_attempt` runs the job's `run` under the global 30s timeout and a successful return ends the job (src/worker.py:11-12 `def run_attempt(job): return job.run(timeout=TIMEOUT_S)`)
records.proposal: The worker pulls a job from the head and calls `run_attempt`, which runs `job.run` under the global `TIMEOUT_S` (30s) budget; a normal return ends the job (`src/worker.py:11-12`).

records.evidence: docs/reference/queue-walkthrough.md:10-15 — six narrative sentences whose checkable content fits one line: on `JobError`, `handle_failure` increments `attempts` and either drops at `MAX_ATTEMPTS` or re-enters at the tail (src/worker.py:15-20), plus the tail-spacing rationale
records.proposal: On `JobError`, `handle_failure` increments `job.attempts`; at `MAX_ATTEMPTS` (5) the job is dropped outright, otherwise it re-enters at the tail (`src/worker.py:15-20`) — tail re-entry spaces retries by roughly the queue's drain time, letting transient failures clear.
